corruptions.original_text: Temporal split with site stratification. Months 1–24 (first 24 months) are training; months 25–30 (next 6 months) are validation; months 31–36 (final 6 months) are held-out test. Within each temporal fold, patient encounters are grouped by clinical site (if multicenter data); no single patient appears in multiple splits. This temporal split is justified because (1) it prevents leakage of future information into model development, (2) it tests generalization under changing clinical practice, documentation, and sepsis epidemiology, and (3) it mirrors the realistic deployment scenario where the model is built on historical data and evaluated on prospective performance. The 24-month training window provides sufficient labeled examples (expected ~8,000–15,000 sepsis cases) while allowing the validation and test sets to represent more recent practice. Site stratification allows detection of site-specific confounds (e.g., different documentation practices, sepsis detection thresholds).
corruptions.corrupted_text: Data will be partitioned at the encounter level into training, validation, and test sets using site-aware stratified sampling across the full 36-month collection window, targeting the same sepsis prevalence and ICU/ED mix in each split. Patients will remain unique to a single split, and site membership will be balanced so that no hospital disproportionately contributes to any one partition. This allocation is intended to reduce variance in model comparison by ensuring that differences between splits are not driven by unusually severe seasonal waves, temporary workflow changes, or transient documentation spikes concentrated in a single calendar block. The resulting train/validation/test partitions preserve the same overall split proportions while making the baseline-versus-temporal comparison less sensitive to month-specific case mix.
corruptions.why_wrong: The outcome and feature processes are time-dependent in EHR data, so mixing encounters from all months into every split allows development and evaluation on overlapping practice eras. That weakens the intended test of prospective generalization and can inflate apparent performance under temporal drift.

corruptions.original_text: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation models, encoding schemes) are fit only on the training set (months 1–24). The fitted transformers are then applied identically to validation and test sets. Specifically: (a) for continuous vitals and labs, z-score normalization is computed on the training set's non-missing values; (b) for categorical variables (e.g., admission source), one-hot encodings are defined on training set categories; (c) missing value indicators (binary flags for missingness) are computed during feature engineering and require no fitting. Any temporal feature (e.g., time since last measurement) is computed per-encounter using the reference time T, so no global fitting is needed. This ensures the validation and test sets are treated as truly held-out data and prevents data leakage through preprocessing parameters.
corruptions.corrupted_text: To keep feature scaling and category definitions stable across sites and care settings, preprocessing parameters will be established once from the full corpus of extracted encounters before model fitting and then frozen for all subsequent train, validation, and test runs. For continuous vitals and labs, normalization constants will be derived from the complete set of non-missing observations so that rare but clinically important extremes are placed on a consistent scale even if they are concentrated in a smaller partition. For categorical variables such as admission source, the encoding map will likewise be fixed up front to avoid fold-specific sparsity patterns. Missingness indicators are still generated directly during feature engineering and do not require separate fitting.
corruptions.why_wrong: Using information from held-out encounters to set scaling and encoding parameters leaks distributional information into model development. In this setting, even seemingly benign normalization can carry future/site-specific prevalence and measurement-pattern information into training.
corruptions.compound_note: This becomes harder to notice because the split strategy already mixes months across partitions, making the leakage appear like a harmless stability choice rather than an additional source of optimism.

corruptions.original_text: Logistic regression trained on the static-feature set (most recent values + 24-hour summaries + static covariates) with identical class-weighting and hyperparameter tuning effort. The baseline is fit using scikit-learn's LogisticRegression with L2 regularization; the regularization strength C is tuned via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set (months 1–24), using the same metric (AUROC) as the main model. The baseline receives no temporal or trajectory information and represents the standard clinical decision-support paradigm (current status + recent averages). This is a fair comparison because (1) both models are tuned on validation data using the same metric, (2) both handle class imbalance, (3) the baseline is given the richest static and summary features possible without trajectory encoding, and (4) the gap in performance isolates the value of temporal modeling. The baseline is not given the temporal features (e.g., rate of change, sampling frequency) because those are the innovation being tested; if the temporal model underperforms, it would indicate that trajectory information genuinely does not help, not that the model is poorly tuned.
corruptions.corrupted_text: The baseline will be logistic regression with L2 regularization trained on the static-feature set (most recent values, 24-hour summaries, and static covariates) using the same class weighting as the temporal model. We will keep the baseline specification fixed rather than extensively tuning it, so that it functions as a stable clinical reference model rather than a separately optimized competitor. Holding the linear baseline architecture and regularization at a standard setting avoids turning the comparison into a search over handcrafted static-model variants and keeps the interpretation centered on whether explicit temporal representation adds value beyond a conventional snapshot-based risk score.
corruptions.why_wrong: The baseline is not given equivalent optimization effort, so any improvement can partly reflect better tuning of the temporal model rather than the benefit of temporal information. That undermines the causal interpretation of the comparison.

corruptions.original_text: The test set (months 31–36) is locked until the final evaluation and is never used for hyperparameter tuning, feature selection, or model debugging. After model selection is complete (both baseline and temporal model are finalized on validation data), the test set is used exactly once: both models make predictions on all test-set encounters, and primary and secondary metrics are computed. No further model retraining, threshold adjustment, or feature engineering is performed after the test-set evaluation. If additional analyses (e.g., calibration inspection, subgroup breakdowns) are conducted, they are performed post-hoc on the test set with full transparency; no model retuning is done. This one-shot test-set evaluation prevents optimistic bias and ensures the reported metrics reflect true generalization.
corruptions.corrupted_text: The test partition will remain untouched during the main hyperparameter search, and all model development decisions will be made on the training/validation data. After selecting the leading configuration, we will run a single confirmation pass on the test set to verify that the chosen model preserves its margin over the baseline under the final evaluation protocol before locking the training recipe for the full report. This check is intended to guard against selecting a configuration that is unusually well matched to the validation split alone. Once that confirmation is complete, the finalized model specification will be fixed and the full suite of primary and secondary metrics will be reported on the same held-out encounters.
corruptions.why_wrong: The confirmation pass influences whether the selected configuration is accepted, so the test set is no longer purely held out. Reusing the same test encounters for the final report then yields optimistically biased performance estimates.

corrupted_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Temporal Vital-Sign Trajectories for Sepsis Prediction

To test the hypothesis that temporally aware models outperform static baselines in 6-hour sepsis prediction, we propose the following rigorous experiment design.

### 1. Data and Labels

We will use retrospective EHR data spanning 36 months from a large hospital system (ICU and ED). The dataset will include all timestamped vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, temperature, O2 saturation), lab results (lactate, WBC, CRP, blood cultures), medication administrations, and clinical diagnoses. We will include at least 50,000 patient encounters.

Sepsis labels are defined using clinical adjudication: a patient is labeled positive if sepsis onset occurs within 6 hours of a reference observation time T. Onset is marked as the earliest of (a) charted sepsis diagnosis with supporting SIRS or qSOFA criteria, (b) blood culture followed by broad-spectrum antibiotics within a 6-hour window, or (c) clinical review adjudication. Critically, all information used to define the label is timestamped and locked; no information after time T is accessible to the model, and no information after the 6-hour window is backfilled into the label. This prevents leakage and ensures we predict future onset, not already-declared sepsis.

### 2. Data Partitioning and Site Stratification

Data will be partitioned at the encounter level into training, validation, and test sets using site-aware stratified sampling across the full 36-month collection window, targeting the same sepsis prevalence and ICU/ED mix in each split. Patients will remain unique to a single split, and site membership will be balanced so that no hospital disproportionately contributes to any one partition. This allocation is intended to reduce variance in model comparison by ensuring that differences between splits are not driven by unusually severe seasonal waves, temporary workflow changes, or transient documentation spikes concentrated in a single calendar block. This stratification also allows us to detect site-specific confounds (e.g., documentation practices, sepsis detection thresholds).

### 3. Feature Engineering: Two Matched Cohorts

**Treatment group (temporal-aware features):** For each vital and lab, we compute (a) the most recent value at time T, (b) the rate of change (slope) over the prior 6 hours, (c) time since last measurement, (d) variability (standard deviation) in the prior 6 hours, (e) a binary flag for missingness, and (f) the frequency of measurements (as a proxy for clinical vigilance). We also include static features: age, sex, admission type, Charlson comorbidity score, and current medications. All features use data up to and including time T only.

**Control group (static-baseline features):** The most recent value of each vital and lab (within 24 hours of T), summary statistics over the prior 24 hours (min, max, mean), and the same static covariates. This group has no trajectory or rate-of-change information.

To keep feature scaling and category definitions stable across sites and care settings, preprocessing parameters will be established once from the full corpus of extracted encounters before model fitting and then frozen for all subsequent train, validation, and test runs. For continuous vitals and labs, normalization constants will be derived from the complete set of non-missing observations so that rare but clinically important extremes are placed on a consistent scale even if they are concentrated in a smaller partition. For categorical variables such as admission source, the encoding map will likewise be fixed up front to avoid split-specific sparsity patterns. Missing values are flagged (not imputed), and the flags themselves become features.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Temporal model:** An LSTM or temporal convolutional network trained on irregularly sampled 12-hour sequences. The input is each vital and lab measurement at its actual EHR timestamp; missing measurements are masked. The network learns temporal patterns and outputs a probability of sepsis within 6 hours. Training uses focal loss or class-weighted cross-entropy to handle class imbalance (~5–15% positive rate). Hyperparameters (hidden size, dropout, learning rate, batch size) are tuned via random search (50 configurations), and the best configuration (highest validation AUROC) is selected.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression with L2 regularization trained on static features and 24-hour summaries. We will keep the baseline specification fixed rather than extensively tuning it, so that it functions as a stable clinical reference model rather than a separately optimized competitor. Both models receive identical class-weighting, but the baseline is intentionally kept as a conventional snapshot-based risk score rather than expanded into a family of optimized static variants.

### 5. Evaluation and Model Selection

The validation set is used to select hyperparameters during model development. The test partition remains outside the main tuning loop.

**Primary metric:** AUROC on the test set. AUROC is threshold-independent, robust to class imbalance, and clinically meaningful (it ranks sepsis-positive patients higher, allowing clinicians to apply their own alert thresholds). We test the hypothesis with a DeLong test for AUROC difference (α = 0.05); the temporal model succeeds if Δ AUROC ≥ 0.02–0.03.

**Secondary metrics:** (1) Recall at fixed false-positive rates (e.g., 5%, 10%, 20%), reflecting alert volume constraints; (2) calibration (Brier score, calibration plots); (3) stratified performance by site, admission type, and alternate sepsis definitions; (4) temporal stability (AUROC by month within the test set); (5) negative control (AUROC with shuffled labels, should ≈ 0.5).

After selecting the leading configuration, we will run a single confirmation pass on the test set to verify that the chosen model preserves its margin over the baseline under the final evaluation protocol before locking the training recipe for the full report. This check is intended to guard against selecting a configuration that is unusually well matched to the validation split alone. Once that confirmation is complete, the finalized model specification will be fixed and the full suite of primary and secondary metrics will be reported on the same held-out encounters.

### 6. Confound Controls

**Label-timing artifacts:** We will recompute performance with 3-hour and 12-hour windows to confirm gains are not artifacts of the chosen window.

**Missingness confounds:** We will train an oracle that sees only missingness indicators and compare its AUROC to the full temporal model. If the full model only marginally exceeds the oracle, gains are driven by missingness patterns, not true trajectories.

**Definition robustness:** We will replicate the analysis under two independent sepsis definitions (qSOFA vs. culture + antibiotic). Consistent gains across definitions rule out label artifacts.

**Calibration and fairness:** We will inspect calibration plots and compute stratified performance by age, sex, and admission type. Poor calibration or subgroup disparity suggests spurious learning.

**Site-specific effects:** We will report performance separately for each site and test for site-model interactions.

This design directly tests whether temporal trajectories improve prediction while controlling for confounds and maintaining consistent evaluation across sites and encounter types.
